documentRead.whatItIs: A practical how-to guide from the Center for Constitutional Rights on using FOIA and state open records laws to support activism and organizing.
documentRead.audience: Activists, organizers, and social movements — plus lawyers who support them.
documentRead.coreMessage: Anyone can use FOIA to pry loose government records for a campaign — but it's slow, so plan your strategy, write your request carefully, and be ready to appeal or litigate.

stats.fact: NDLON v. ICE ultimately produced over 300,000 pages of information on the Secure Communities program.
The NDLON v. ICE litigation took over three years to complete.
FOIA requires federal agencies to determine and notify a requester of their response within 20 business days.
You have 90 days to appeal any determination made by a federal agency.
Federal agencies can invoke nine statutory exemptions to redact or withhold documents.

stories.subject: NDLON v. ICE (Secure Communities campaign)
stories.whatHappened: The National Day Laborer Organizing Network used FOIA to expose how ICE's Secure Communities program worked, winning 300,000+ pages after 3+ years of litigation and a landmark ruling from Judge Shira Scheindlin.

stories.subject: St. Charles Parish Sheriff records request (Dakota Access Pipeline)
stories.whatHappened: CCR filed a narrow 2017 state records request about the sheriff's trip to observe DAPL protests, received some documents within weeks, then litigated for more over the following year.

stories.subject: Color of Change surveillance request
stories.whatHappened: CCR and Color of Change filed a FOIA about DHS/FBI surveillance of Movement for Black Lives activists, deliberately telling a story in the request itself as an advocacy tool.

stories.subject: Detention Watch Network — 'Banking on Detention' report
stories.whatHappened: Using information produced by their FOIA request, DWN and CCR released the 2015 report 'Banking on Detention.'

stories.subject: Herman Ferguson records requests
stories.whatHappened: CCR's early-1990s public records requests supporting activist Herman Ferguson revealed the extent to which the FBI and NYPD were surveilling him and his organization.

stories.subject: Louisiana Bucket Brigade / Bayou Bridge Pipeline
stories.whatHappened: Director Anne Rolfes described how her group and grassroots partners filed a state records request seeking the truth about a new pipeline threatening land, water, and health.

reasons.length: At ~8,073 words of dense practical how-to across many sections, a middle-length page fits — enough room to lay out the process without dumping the whole manual.
reasons.summaryChoice: The doc has a genuine ~833-word intro/overview written by the authors, so lead with the exec summary rather than a synthetic one.
reasons.coverLayout: It's a text-heavy resource guide with no strong hero image, so a text-forward layout suits it best.

aiSummary.note: The doc already has a solid authored overview — use AI only if you want a punchier one-liner.

execSummary.note: The 833-word intro clearly states what FOIA is, who this is for, and why it matters — lean on it.

highlights.note: A few concrete numbers here — 300,000 pages, 3 years, 20-day and 90-day clocks — give readers something to stop for.

findings.note: This is a process guide, so pull out the actionable takeaways: how to write, file, track, appeal, and litigate a request.

toc.note: The guide has clear, logical stages (before you begin, writing, admin process, reviewing, litigating) — a TOC helps readers navigate.

storytelling.note: Real cases carry this — NDLON v. ICE is vivid and specific, backed by several other named campaigns.

download.note: It's a reference guide people will want to keep — offer the full PDF prominently.

secondary.note: Point to CCR's Open Records Project site and trainings, which the doc explicitly mentions.

share.note: Useful for a movement audience but not central; keep it low-key.
